content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 8,
1993
Honorable Pat Miranda
Councilmeinber
City of Irwindale
5050 North Irwindale Avenue
Irwindale,
CA
91706
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-454
Dear Councilmember Miranda:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning your duties as an Irwindale City Councilmember under
the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Political Reforin Act
(the “Act”)
1
QUESTION
May you participate in a decision regarding the adjustment of
a property line which is currently located more than
3’OO feet from
property you own,
and is proposed to be adjusted to an existing
block wall which is also more than 300 feet from your property?
CONCLUSION
You may participate in the property line adjustment decision
so long as the decision will not foreseeably increase or decrease
the fair market value of your real property by $10,000 or more,
or
the rental value of your property by at least $1,000 in a 12-month
period.
FACTS
The Irwindale City Council will be considering a proposal to
adjust the rear lot line of property fronting on Juarez Street
(to
the north)
and a 6-foot block wall
(to the south).
According to
your facts,
as a result of recent construction that took place in
the area, the city determined that the rear lot line
(to the
south)
as recognized by owners of parcels was incorrect.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Section
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-93-454
Page
2
According to the materials you submitted,
a survey was made
of the property in 1923,
which incorrectly found the rear lot line
to the south was approximately 18-feet further south than the
actual deed line.
The incorrect line was treated as the actual
rear lot line by the property owners and city from that time
forward.
Subsequently,
in the early 70’s a 6-foot block wall was
built approximately
5 feet further south of the first erroneous
property line and the owners of the parcels recognized the wall as
their southern property line.
The city will now be considering corrective action with
respect to the lot line.
City staff has suggested recognizing the
wall as the property of the owners of the parcels to the north,
and establishing the rear lot line six inches south of the wall.
You own several parcels that are in the jurisdiction and near
the site of the property line dispute.
1.
You own two adjacent lots
(one is your residence, the
other is a rental)
421 feet north of the wall.
Your property is
separated from the wall by two streets and three rows of houses.
2.
You own two rental adjacent parcels 553 feet’ northeast of
the wall on Irwindale Avenue.
Irwindale Avenue intersects with
Juarez Street where the subject parcels are located.
3.
You own a rental parcel 576 feet southeast of the wall on
Irwindale Avenue.
Irwindale Avenue intersects with Juarez Street
where the subject parcels are located.
You have asked whether you may participate in the decision
despite your real property interest.
ANALYSIS
Conflict of Interest
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making,
or otherwise using his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that an official has
a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally,
on the official or on a member of his or her immediate
family,
or on:
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1000)
or more.
Section 87103(b).
You stated you own several parcels in the jurisdiction.
We
assume that you have an interest in each of these properties of


Our File No.
A—93-454
Page
3
$1,000 or more.
Consequently,
you are prohibited from making,
participating in,
or influencing decisions which would have a
reasonably foreseeable
2 material financial effect on your property
that is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
Regulation 18702.3 provides that the effect of a decision on
real property in which an official has an economic interest is
material if:
(3)
The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of .300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries
(or
the proposed boundaries)
of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an
interest; or
(B)
Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per
12 month
period.
Each parcel you own is within 2,500 feet of the existing and
proposed property line.
However,
none of the properties is within
300 feet of the existing and proposed property lines.
Consequently,
you may not participate in the decision regarding
the property line if the decision will foreseeably increase or
decrease the fair market value of your real property by $10,000 or
more or the rental value of your property by at least $1,000 in a
12—month period.
(Regulation 18702.3(a) (3) (A).)
Regulation 18702.3(d)
sets forth factors that must be
considered in determining whether a decision will have a material
financial effect on your real property.
3
These factors are as
follows:
1.
The proximity of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the magnitude of the
proposed project or change in use in re1aiionship
2
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(j.fl
re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
The factors to be considered are not limited to the factors
specified in Regulation 18702.3(d).


Our File No.
A—93—454
Page
4
to the property in which the official has an
interest;
2.
Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the property;
3.
[W)hether it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will result in a change to the
character of the neighborhood including,
but not
limited to, the effect on traffic,
view, privacy,
intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or
similar traits of the neighborhood.
While we cannot determine whether the decision will have such
an effect on your real property, it appears from your facts that
such an effect is unlikely.
(1)
None of your parcels are closer than 400 feet of the
existing or proposed boundaries.
(2)
None of your parcels adjoin the lots subject to the
decision.
In fact, the parcels closest to the site of the
decision are separated from the site by two streets and three rows
of houses.
(3)
The use of the property subject to the decision will not
be changed.
The wall already exists and parcel owners have been
using the wall as their boundary prior to 1975.
However,
we must leave this ultimate factual determination of
materiality to you.
4
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
John W.
Wallace
Counsel,
Legal Division
This letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ogelsby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
1 Counsel
